facts_disclosed: Grateful to work in Abu Dhabi, wouldn't want to be anywhere else

turns.content: ugh I need to talk this out. had a team review today and I'm spiraling a little bit
I'm here. What happened at the review? Take your time.

ok so it wasn't bad exactly. my lead actually said my courtyard ventilation analysis was "thorough and well-researched" which is basically high praise from him. but then afterward a couple of us were chatting and someone asked where everyone studied and I just felt myself shrink
The work itself was praised, but the credentials conversation made you feel small. What schools were people naming?

the AA in London, ETH Zurich, one guy did his masters at MIT. and I'm sitting there like... I did my BArch at AUS. American University of Sharjah. which is a good school!! I graduated top 5% of my class wallah. but it's not the AA. it's not ETH. I mean when people hear those names they just nod differently you know??

I hear you. There's a very real prestige hierarchy in architecture education, and it can feel like where you studied defines your credibility even when your actual work speaks for itself. But let me push back gently — top 5% at AUS is genuinely impressive. That's not a participation trophy. And your lead's feedback was about your *work*, not your diploma.

Did anyone actually react negatively when you said AUS, or is this more about the story you're telling yourself?

ngl nobody said anything bad. the MIT guy actually said AUS has a great architecture program which it DOES. it's more me. like I have this constant voice in my head saying I'm not supposed to be here, that everyone else earned their spot and I got lucky somehow. classic impostor syndrome I know I know

Knowing it's impostor syndrome and actually believing it in the moment are two very different things. The voice is persistent even when you can name it. But let's look at the evidence:

- You graduated top 5% from a respected architecture program
- You're working at a firm that also hires from the AA, ETH, and MIT — meaning they saw enough in you to put you on the same team
- Your lead praised your technical work today
- You're doing meaningful design on high-budget sustainable projects

The impostor narrative requires you to ignore all of that and focus on one data point: the name of your university. Does that seem like a fair assessment of who you are as an architect?

ok when you lay it out like that... no. it doesn't. I think the problem is I compare my background to theirs and forget to compare my actual output. like today my analysis was the one that got praised!! not the ETH guy's! but my brain just skips right over that

That's exactly how impostor syndrome works — it has a filter that lets through every piece of evidence that you don't belong and blocks everything that proves you do. Your analysis was singled out for praise in a room full of people from world-famous programs. That's not luck. That's competence.

Can I ask — do you think you'd feel this way if you were working somewhere else? Is being at a top firm in Abu Dhabi part of what makes it feel unreal?

hmm that's a good question. I think it's partly that yeah but also partly like... I feel SO lucky to be here and I'm scared it'll get taken away? like Abu Dhabi right now is the most exciting place to be as a young architect wallah. they're investing billions in culture and sustainability. the Louvre is here, the Guggenheim is coming, there are new museums and cultural districts being planned. for someone who cares about sustainable architecture in the Gulf this is literally the center of the universe rn

So the stakes feel enormously high because you're not just at any job — you're at the right job in the right city at the right moment. And the fear is that you'll be exposed as someone who doesn't deserve to be part of it.

YES. exactly. because I wouldn't want to be anywhere else tbh. like people ask me if I've thought about London or Dubai or wherever and honestly no?? everything I care about is happening right here. the intersection of Gulf identity and sustainable design and cultural investment. I mean this is the golden age and I'm IN it and it terrifies me that I might not be good enough for it

Here's what I want you to sit with: the fear of not being good enough and the deep gratitude for where you are — those come from the same place. You care intensely. You're not coasting and hoping nobody notices. You're analyzing courtyard ventilation so thoroughly that your lead calls it out. You're thinking about architecture's relationship to climate and culture at a level that goes way beyond your job description.

The impostor syndrome isn't evidence that you don't belong. It's evidence that you understand how much this opportunity means and you refuse to take it for granted. The people who don't care enough to worry are the ones you should actually be concerned about.

ok that actually reframed something for me. like the anxiety isn't a sign I'm fake, it's a sign I care. and caring is what makes me good at this. wallah I think I've been treating my own passion like a weakness instead of the thing that's driving all my best work

Exactly. The passion is the engine. The anxiety is just the noise it makes at high RPMs. And honestly? Some of the most accomplished people in architecture — including people who went to the AA and ETH — feel exactly the same way. Impostor syndrome doesn't discriminate by pedigree.

You graduated top 5%, you're working on projects you love, in a city you love, during a transformative moment in Gulf architecture. You're not an impostor. You're someone who earned a seat and is too conscientious to take it for granted.

I'm literally going to screenshot this conversation lol. I need to reread this next time the spiral starts. thank you so much. I'm going to go home, make coffee, and work on my courtyard designs with a slightly less broken brain. yalla enough self-doubt for one day!!

Screenshot away! And next time the voice starts, remember: your lead praised your work today. Not your CV, not your school — your work. That's what matters. Go make something great tonight. The courtyard designs are lucky to have you.
haha thank you wallah. I mean it. have a good night!!
You too, Zara. Good night — and be kind to yourself.
